mini_headings_covered: What is the benefit of physics for society?
What are the technologies directly related to physics that benefit society?
Explain influence of physics on society in transport, health, economy, technology
Explain influence of physics on society in economy
To promote physics education and research
What are the benefits of physics communities?
Supermassive black hole at galactic center

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best explains how physics contributes to economic growth through technological infrastructure?

options.A: Physics provides trained personnel for science and engineering sectors
options.B: Physics eliminates the need for all other sciences
options.C: Physics only benefits theoretical research with no practical applications
options.D: Physics focuses exclusively on military weapons development

questions.hint: Consider how educated workers in STEM fields drive innovation and industry.
questions.explanation: Physics education produces skilled scientists and engineers who develop new technologies, driving economic growth. Option B is false; physics complements other sciences. Option C contradicts known applications.

source_trace.content_anchor: Physics contributes to the technological infrastructure and provides trained personnel needed to take advantage of scientific advances

explanations_tiered.definition: Technological infrastructure refers to the physical and organizational systems (transport, energy, communication) that enable economic activity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Physics-trained personnel develop and maintain advanced manufacturing, telecommunications, and energy systems, increasing productivity and economic output.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Countries with more physics-trained people build better roads, internet, and power plants. These things help businesses grow and create jobs.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Physics education produces engineers and technicians who design fiber-optic networks, semiconductor fabs, and renewable energy systems, forming the backbone of a modern economy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Human capital in STEM has a positive elasticity of output: a 1% increase in physics-trained workforce correlates with ~0.5% GDP growth in developed economies, per endogenous growth theory.
reasoning_steps: Identify how physics-trained people contribute to industry.
Link skilled workforce to technological development.
Recognize that infrastructure requires specialized knowledge.
misconceptions: Some think physics is only academic. In fact, physics graduates work in industry, finance, and engineering, driving economic growth.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Physics → trained personnel → better infrastructure → economic growth.

glossary_entry.definition: The physical and organizational systems (energy, transport, communication) that enable economic productivity.

flashcard.front: How does physics training help the economy?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Trained physicists build and maintain technological infrastructure, boosting productivity and growth.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is one way physics contributes to economic growth?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Training personnel who build technological infrastructure (energy, transport, communications).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Physical systems enabling economic activity, developed by physics-trained workers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss how countries with strong STEM education have higher GDP growth.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the importance of physics to society
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Trained personnel (physicists/engineers) build infrastructure; other options are false or incomplete.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A lever with a mechanical advantage of 4 requires an input force of 50 N to lift a load. What is the weight of the load?
questions.hint: Mechanical advantage = load force / input force.
questions.explanation: Mechanical advantage (MA) = load force / input force. With MA = 4 and input force = 50 N, load force = MA × input = 4 × 50 N = 200 N. Option A divides incorrectly.

source_trace.content_anchor: Simple mechanical devices such as pulleys and levers

explanations_tiered.definition: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of output force (load) to input force (effort) for a simple machine.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Levers multiply force: a small input force can lift a larger load. MA = load/effort. Here, load = MA × effort.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A lever with MA=4 means you push with 1 unit of force and lift 4 units. Push with 50 N, you lift 200 N.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From MA = F_load / F_input, rearranging gives F_load = MA × F_input = 4 × 50 N = 200 N.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a lever, MA = distance from fulcrum to effort / distance from fulcrum to load. This torque balance: \(F_{\text{input}} d_1 = F_{\text{load}} d_2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Recall MA = load force / input force.
reasoning_steps: Write the formula for mechanical advantage.
Substitute known values.
Multiply to find load force.
misconceptions: Some divide incorrectly: 50/4 = 12.5 N (option A). Remember: MA > 1 means load > input.

glossary_entry.definition: Ratio of output force to input force for a simple machine; values >1 indicate force multiplication.

flashcard.front: A lever has MA=4 and input=50 N. What load can it lift?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you calculate load force from mechanical advantage?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a real lever or seesaw to demonstrate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the importance of physics to society
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Load = MA × Input = 4 × 50 N = 200 N; 50/4 = 12.5 N (A) is incorrect division.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.structural_anchor: What are the benefits of physics communities?
questions.question: Why do physics communities such as EPS or EPSNA enhance physics education and research in a country?

options.A: They organize conferences and exchange scientific information
options.C: They only focus on commercial applications of physics
options.D: They restrict physics knowledge to members only

questions.hint: Physics communities connect professionals and share knowledge through meetings and publications.
questions.explanation: Physics communities promote education and research by organizing conferences, disseminating information, and supporting curriculum development. They complement, not replace, universities (B).

source_trace.structural_element: What are the benefits of physics communities?
source_trace.content_anchor: To organize and coordinate various conferences on physics education and exchange of scientific information

explanations_tiered.definition: Physics communities are professional organizations that connect physicists to share research, organize events, and promote the field.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: By facilitating conferences, journals, and curriculum input, physics communities accelerate research dissemination and improve education quality.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Physics communities are like clubs for physicists. They hold meetings where scientists share discoveries and help each other teach better.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Annual conferences allow presentation of peer-reviewed research. Workshops train teachers. Journals disseminate findings. These activities directly enhance the field.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Professional societies (APS, EPS) publish high-impact journals (Physical Review, etc.), organize international collaborations, and advocate for science funding — all accelerating knowledge production.
reasoning_steps: Identify activities of physics communities: conferences, publications, outreach.
Recognize that sharing information advances education and research.
Eliminate options that limit or replace existing institutions.
misconceptions: Some think physics communities are secretive. They are open professional networks that share knowledge widely.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Physics communities: conferences + publications + outreach = better research & education.

glossary_entry.definition: An organized group of physicists working together to advance research, education, and public understanding of physics.

flashcard.front: How do physics communities help education?
flashcard.flashcard_back: They organize conferences, share teaching resources, and provide professional development.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is one way physics communities improve research?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Organizing conferences where scientists share and review new discoveries.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Professional organization advancing physics through conferences, publications, and education.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Ask students to find a physics community near them (e.g., EPS, APS).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss physics communities and their roles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Conferences and information exchange are key benefits; communities don't replace universities (B).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The statement \(2 + 2 = 4\) is known without sense experience. This type of knowledge is called:
questions.hint: A priori knowledge comes from reason, not from experience.
questions.explanation: A priori knowledge is known independently of experience, such as mathematical truths. A posteriori knowledge (B) comes from experience. Sensory perception (C) is a type of a posteriori knowledge.

source_trace.content_anchor: Usual examples of non- experiential knowledge are mathematics and logic. Take, for example, 2 + 2 = 4

explanations_tiered.definition: A priori knowledge is knowledge that can be justified independently of sense experience, typically through reason or logic.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mathematical truths and logical tautologies are classic a priori. They are necessary truths, true in all possible worlds, not contingent on observation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You don't need to count apples to know 2+2=4. You can just think about it — that's a priori knowledge.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Unlike a posteriori knowledge (which requires experiments), a priori truths are known through rational insight. Mathematical axioms are a priori.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Kant distinguished analytic a priori (predicate contained in subject) from synthetic a priori (mathematics, geometry). 2+2=4 is synthetic a priori for Kant.
reasoning_steps: Identify that 2+2=4 is known without counting physical objects.
Recall definition of a priori: independent of experience.
Eliminate a posteriori (experience-based) options.
misconceptions: Some think all knowledge is from experience. Mathematical truths are known by reason, not by measuring physical objects.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: A priori = knowledge from reason (math, logic). A posteriori = knowledge from experience.

glossary_entry.definition: Knowledge that can be justified without sense experience, typically through reason or logic.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Knowledge gained through reason alone, not through sensory experience, e.g., mathematical truths.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Is mathematical knowledge a priori or a posteriori?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A priori — known by reason, not by sensory experience.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Knowledge independent of experience, based on reason.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: A priori = reason-based (math). A posteriori = experience-based.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss: can we know 2+2=4 without ever counting? Yes — it's a priori.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss how scientific knowledge is constructed
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. A priori is knowledge without experience; a posteriori (B) is from experience.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student heats water at four different altitudes and records the boiling temperature at each location. This activity represents which step of the scientific method?
questions.hint: The experiment is the step where data are collected through controlled procedures.
questions.explanation: An experiment is a controlled procedure to test a hypothesis by collecting data. Here, the student systematically collects boiling point data. Peer review (D) occurs after publication.

source_trace.content_anchor: You decide to heat water at different altitudes and record the boiling temperature

explanations_tiered.definition: An experiment is a controlled procedure designed to test a hypothesis by collecting empirical data under specified conditions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Experiments manipulate independent variables (altitude) and measure dependent variables (boiling point) while controlling other factors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Heating water at different places and writing down temperatures is an experiment. You're testing if altitude changes boiling point.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The student performs measurements at different altitudes (independent variable), recording boiling points (dependent variable). This data collection is the experiment step.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Proper experimental design includes controls (same heating rate, same water volume) to isolate the effect of altitude on boiling point, minimizing confounding variables.
reasoning_steps: Identify the action: heating water and recording temperatures.
Recall that collecting data under controlled conditions = experiment.
Eliminate hypothesis (proposed explanation) and conclusion (interpretation).
misconceptions: Some think an experiment only happens in a lab. Field measurements (different altitudes) are also experiments.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Experiment = controlled data collection to test hypothesis.

glossary_entry.definition: A controlled procedure to test a hypothesis by collecting empirical data under specific conditions.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between a hypothesis and an experiment?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Hypothesis is a proposed explanation; experiment is the data-collection test of that hypothesis.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What step involves collecting data by heating water at different altitudes?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Experiment — controlled data collection to test a hypothesis.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Controlled procedure collecting data to test a hypothesis.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Experiment = data collection test of hypothesis.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students design their own altitude-boiling point experiment.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Discuss how scientific knowledge is constructed
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Collecting data = experiment; hypothesis (A) is before, conclusion (C) is after.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
questions.subsection: Physics careers in space and astronomy
questions.question: Which astronomy-related task would a physicist most likely perform?

options.A: Collecting data from global satellites and operating telescopes

questions.hint: Astronomers use telescopes and satellite data to study celestial objects.
questions.explanation: Astronomers collect data from satellites and telescopes. Surgery (B) is medical, contracts (C) legal, fashion (D) design — none involve astronomy or physics.

source_trace.section_anchor: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Physics careers in space and astronomy
source_trace.content_anchor: collecting data from global satellites and spacecraft and operating radio and optical telescopes

explanations_tiered.definition: Astronomy is the scientific study of celestial objects, space, and the physical universe as a whole.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Astronomers use telescopes (radio, optical, infrared) and space-based observatories to collect data on stars, planets, galaxies, and cosmic phenomena.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Astronomers look through big telescopes or use satellite pictures to learn about stars and planets. They don't do surgery or write legal papers.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Tasks include operating radio telescopes for pulsar timing, reducing CCD images of galaxies, and analyzing exoplanet transit light curves from satellites like TESS.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Astronomers apply radiative transfer, N-body simulations, and interferometry to interpret data. The signal-to-noise ratio \(\mathrm{SNR} = S/\sqrt{S+B}\) guides observation design.
reasoning_steps: Recall what astronomers study: stars, planets, galaxies.
Identify tasks involving telescopes or satellite data.
Eliminate non-astronomy activities (surgery, law, fashion).
misconceptions: Some think astronomy is just star-gazing. Professional astronomers use complex data analysis, spectroscopy, and computational models.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Astronomer: operates telescopes, analyzes satellite data.

glossary_entry.definition: A scientist who studies celestial objects, space, and the universe using telescopes and satellite data.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Collects satellite data and operates telescopes to study stars, planets, and galaxies.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What equipment does an astronomer use?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Telescopes (optical, radio) and satellite data collection systems.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Scientist studying celestial objects via telescopes and satellites.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show images of radio telescopes and Hubble/JWST images.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Telescopes and satellite data are astronomy tasks; surgery (B) is medical.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
questions.question: A solar panel receives \(500\,\mathrm{W/m^2}\) of sunlight. If the panel has an area of \(2\,\mathrm{m^2}\) and an efficiency of 15\%, what electrical power does it produce?
questions.explanation: Incident power = \(500\,\mathrm{W/m^2} \times 2\,\mathrm{m^2} = 1000\,\mathrm{W}\). Efficiency 15% = 0.15. Output = \(1000 \times 0.15 = 150\,\mathrm{W}\). Option A uses 75 = 500×0.15 forgetting area.

source_trace.section_anchor: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness

explanations_tiered.definition: Solar panel efficiency η is the ratio of electrical power output to incident solar power.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Total incident power = intensity × area. Electrical output = η × incident power. This calculation applies to photovoltaic energy conversion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: First find total sunlight hitting the panel: 500 W per m² times 2 m² = 1000 W. Then take 15% of that: 150 W.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Incident power: \(P_{\text{in}} = I \cdot A = 500 \times 2 = 1000\,\mathrm{W}\). Output: \(P_{\text{out}} = \eta P_{\text{in}} = 0.15 \times 1000 = 150\,\mathrm{W}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Efficiency accounts for losses: reflection, non-ideal quantum efficiency, resistive losses. \(\eta = \frac{V_{\text{oc}} I_{\text{sc}} \mathrm{FF}}{P_{\text{in}}}\), where FF is fill factor.
reasoning_steps: Multiply intensity by area to get incident power.
Multiply incident power by efficiency (as decimal).
Report output power in watts.
misconceptions: Some forget to multiply by area: 500 × 0.15 = 75 W (option A). Always include area first.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Solar panel output (W) = η × I × A.

glossary_entry.definition: Fraction of incident solar power converted to electrical power, typically 15–22% for commercial panels.

flashcard.front: How do you calculate solar panel electrical output?
flashcard.flashcard_back: P_out = η × I × A (efficiency × intensity × area).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A 2 m² solar panel at 500 W/m² with 15% efficiency: output power?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Fraction of incident solar power converted to electricity.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss why efficiency matters for rooftop solar.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain job opportunities in energy sector
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Incident = 500×2=1000 W; output = 0.15×1000=150 W; option A forgets area.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
questions.question: A meteorologist uses weather balloon data to predict tomorrow's maximum temperature. This prediction relies on which physics-based model?
questions.hint: Weather involves air motion (fluid dynamics) and heat transfer (thermodynamics).
questions.explanation: Weather prediction uses numerical weather prediction models based on fluid dynamics (Navier-Stokes) and thermodynamics. Plate tectonics (B) studies Earth's crust, not daily weather.

source_trace.section_anchor: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
source_trace.content_anchor: meteorologists focus on areas such as daily weather forecasting, as well as researching the long- term effects of climate change

explanations_tiered.definition: Atmospheric fluid dynamics describes the motion of air masses using the Navier-Stokes equations; thermodynamics governs heat transfer and phase changes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Numerical weather prediction solves primitive equations (conservation of mass, momentum, energy) on a 3D grid to forecast temperature, pressure, wind, and precipitation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Weather forecasting is like predicting how air moves and heats up. Physicists use math equations to model wind, clouds, and temperature changes.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The Navier-Stokes equation \(\rho \frac{D\vec{v}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \rho\vec{g} + \mu\nabla^2\vec{v}\) models air flow. The first law of thermodynamics tracks heat.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Global circulation models (GCMs) discretize the primitive equations on latitude-longitude grids, parameterizing sub-grid processes like convection and radiation for weather prediction.
reasoning_steps: Recall that weather involves motion of air (fluid) and heat transfer.
Identify fluid dynamics and thermodynamics as the relevant physics.
Eliminate plate tectonics (geology), quantum physics, and nuclear physics.
misconceptions: Some think weather is chaotic and unpredictable. While sensitive to initial conditions, ensemble forecasting gives probabilistic predictions.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Weather models: fluid dynamics (air motion) + thermodynamics (heat).

glossary_entry.definition: Forecasting weather by solving fluid dynamics and thermodynamic equations on a computer grid.

flashcard.front: What physics equations do weather forecasts use?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What physics principles drive weather forecasting?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Fluid dynamics (air motion) and thermodynamics (heat transfer).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Computer modeling of atmosphere using fluid dynamics and thermodynamics.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss how supercomputers run weather models.
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: Mission of Physics and Career Awareness
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Weather uses fluid dynamics and thermodynamics; plate tectonics (B) is geology.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What fundamental quantum principle makes quantum cryptography theoretically unbreakable?
questions.hint: Measuring a quantum system inevitably disturbs it, revealing eavesdropping.
questions.explanation: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle (and no-cloning theorem) means that any attempt to measure a quantum state (e.g., photon polarization) changes it, alerting legitimate users. Classical laws (B, C, D) do not provide this security.

source_trace.content_anchor: Quantum cryptography is an application of quantum physics that enhances the security of the information exchange at higher level

explanations_tiered.definition: Quantum cryptography uses quantum mechanical properties (superposition, measurement disturbance) to secure communication, with eavesdropping detection guaranteed by the no-cloning theorem.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In quantum key distribution (BB84 protocol), single photons are sent with random polarizations. Any measurement alters the state, introducing errors that reveal interception.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In quantum cryptography, if someone tries to spy on your message, the act of spying changes the message itself — like opening a letter that self-destructs when read.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle \(\Delta x \Delta p \geq \hbar/2\) implies measurement disturbs the system. Eavesdropping on photon polarization introduces detectable error rates.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The no-cloning theorem states \(\lvert \psi \rangle \otimes \lvert e \rangle \not\to \lvert \psi \rangle \otimes \lvert \psi \rangle\) for unknown states. This prevents undetectable copying, ensuring security.
reasoning_steps: Recall that quantum cryptography detects eavesdropping.
Identify that measurement disturbance is a quantum effect.
Link to Heisenberg's uncertainty principle and no-cloning theorem.
misconceptions: Some think quantum cryptography is totally unbreakable in practice. It is theoretically secure, but implementation vulnerabilities remain.

glossary_entry.definition: Secure communication using quantum mechanics; eavesdropping detection via measurement disturbance.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Measuring a quantum state inevitably changes it, revealing eavesdropping (Heisenberg / no-cloning theorem).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What quantum principle ensures cryptographic security?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Heisenberg's uncertainty principle / no-cloning theorem — measurement disturbs the state.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Secure communication using quantum measurement disturbance to detect eavesdropping.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: QKD security: measure = disturb = detect eavesdropper.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Introduce BB84 with polarized filters demonstration.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Heisenberg's uncertainty (measurement disturbs); classical laws (B,C,D) don't provide this.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why do frontier physics research projects (e.g., LHC, LIGO, JWST) require collaboration among many countries despite high costs?

options.A: No single country has sufficient funding, expertise, and facilities alone
options.B: Physics discoveries are only valid if multiple countries agree
options.D: Countries compete to take sole credit for discoveries

questions.hint: Large-scale physics projects require billions of dollars and specialized expertise from many nations.
questions.explanation: Mega-projects like the LHC cost billions and require diverse expertise (detector design, computing, theory). No single country can provide all resources alone. Option D is contrary to collaborative spirit.

source_trace.content_anchor: Research at the forefront is very expensive (Billions of dollars), it is also needing a collaboration of scientists from many fields. It also involves many countries.

explanations_tiered.definition: International collaboration in physics pools financial, intellectual, and infrastructural resources across countries to achieve goals unattainable individually.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: CERN (LHC), LIGO, and JWST are examples where funding, components, and data analysis are distributed globally, sharing both costs and credit.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Building a huge particle accelerator costs more than any single country can afford. Many countries share the bill and send their best scientists to work together.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The LHC's 27 km tunnel, detectors, and computing grid required contributions from dozens of nations. No single country could supply the €5 billion+ cost alone.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Distributed computing (Worldwide LHC Computing Grid) processes petabytes of data across 170 sites in 42 countries. This model maximizes scientific output per dollar.
reasoning_steps: Recognize that mega-projects cost billions.
Identify that expertise is distributed globally.
Conclude that collaboration shares costs and expertise.
misconceptions: Some think big science could be done by one rich country. Even the US relies on international partners for JWST and LIGO.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Big Physics = international collaboration (shared cost + expertise).

glossary_entry.definition: Large-scale scientific research requiring multinational funding, facilities, and collaboration.

flashcard.front: Why do many countries collaborate on physics megaprojects?
flashcard.flashcard_back: To share enormous costs and combine specialized expertise from around the world.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What drives international collaboration in physics?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: High cost and distributed expertise — no single country can do it alone.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Big Physics = shared cost + shared expertise.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss the role of CERN as a model for global science.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain current status and future of physics
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Cost and expertise are shared; competition for credit (D) is not the primary driver.
